content:
9 min read
Just now

--

So life is nothing but Zero & Won! 😄
Had the absolute privilege of attending ICETCSDP ’26 (International Conference on Emerging Trends in Cyber Security & Digital Protection) at MSIT Kolkata, and obviously couldn’t skip the CTF that came with it.
The theme? Avengers Infinity War 🧤
Every challenge you crack = one Infinity Stone in the bucket. Solve enough, and you basically become Thanos 🫰 (minus the genocide, plus the flags). Let’s snap through this one stone at a time.
1. The Soul Stone Face
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Thanos stood on Vormir and looked upon the Soul Stone’s resting place — a cliff face that had watched civilisations rise and fall. He photographed it. In his arrogance, he stored more than an image. The metadata of the photograph carries a secret older than the stone itself. Look at what the camera recorded, not what the eye sees

• File Provided : vormir.png

• Category : General Skills
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The text was basically screaming at me, “it’s storing more than an image, metadata carries secret.”
Ran exiftool on the image, no fancy tricks needed
exiftool vormir.pngPress enter or click to view image in full size

Straight up found a base64-encoded comment sitting in the metadata.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Decoded it, flag popped out.
First blood, solved within a minute. Easy peasy, lemon squeezy. 🍋
2. ZIP in Disguise
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The Tesseract is a cube of pure energy — yet it fits in a human hand, in a briefcase, in a pocket. It is always more than it appears. This image of the Tesseract is the same. It appears to be a photograph. It is also an archive. The archive is locked. The key is a hash of a word Thanos says.

• File Provided : tesseract.jpg

• Category : Steganography
Name says it all, ZIP in Disguise. File looked like an innocent .jpg, but I wasn't buying it. Skipped opening it entirely and went straight for
but I wasn’t buying it. Skipped opening it entirely and went straight for:
binwalk zip_in_disguise.jpgPress enter or click to view image in full size

And boom, turns out it’s not a JPEG at all, it’s a ZIP archive wearing a JPEG costume. 🥸
Renamed it, tried to extract, and hit a password wall. The challenge hinted at a hash. Cue the guessing game

• INEVIDABLE

• IAMINEVIDABLE

• ✅ SHA1(“INFINITY”)
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Password cracked, extracted flag.txt, flag secured.
3. Alpha Dropper
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The Black Order did not carry weapons openly through the Bifrost. They hid them inside gifts — innocent images, photographs of conquered worlds, shared freely across the network. This PNG is a gift from Proxima Midnight. Its alpha channel carries something that is not transparency. Extract it. Decode it. Inside the noise is a location — a URL the Black Order uses to coordinate attacks across the nine realms.

• File Provided : dropper_m.png

• Category : Forensics
Dropped dropper_m.png straight into Aperi'Solve, it auto-runs zsteg, steghide, outguess, exiftool, binwalk, foremost, and strings, and also spits out a Layers view: every bit-plane (0–7) across R, G, B, and Alpha, rendered separately.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The Layers tab is where it clicked. Flipping through:

• R/G/B bit-planes (any bit): smooth gradients or plain noise 😐

• Alpha bits 1–7: solid black/white, nothing interesting 😐

• Alpha bit 0 (the LSB plane): mostly blank white except a dense, noisy black/white scribble crammed into the top (basically the first row of pixels) 👀
Press enter or click to view image in full size

so I ran
zsteg dropper_m.pngPress enter or click to view image in full size

& got something interesting in the b1,a,lsb,xy position, so I extracted and tried to check the hexdumps
Press enter or click to view image in full size

converted from HEX & applied XOR Brute Force by CyberChef
Press enter or click to view image in full size

and got the FLAG with key = 42
4. Sanctrum of Agamoto
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The Sanctum’s portal protecting the Time Stone appears secure, but hidden flaws in its authentication system allow those who understand the flow of requests to alter reality and gain administrative access. Find the vulnerabilities, claim the stone.

• Target : http://thn12062026.srv.growsoc.com:3000/

• Category : Web Exploitation
This was a simple login page
Press enter or click to view image in full size

so firstly for exploration I created a demo account and tried to capture the request in /register endpoint
Press enter or click to view image in full size

with the request body, and analyzing the leaked source code it was sure that there was a mass assignment vulnerability,
Press enter or click to view image in full size

I requested with an extra field
{
"username":"sagnik17",
"password":"Iamadmin",
"admin":true
}and got the admin user registered successfully
then requested POST into the /admin endpoint
with an additional header (for header bypass)
X-Forwarded-Host: localhostPress enter or click to view image in full size

and got the flag in the response
5. Time Stone : Temporal Container
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A suspicious Docker image was discovered within the Sanctum’s infrastructure shortly before the Time Stone vanished. Intelligence reports suggest it may contain traces left behind by the intruder. Examine the image, uncover what was hidden, and recover the artifact needed to claim the Time Stone.

• File Provided : image.zip

• Category : Forensics
Unzipped the disk image and started poking through the file structure. Went digging in
Press enter or click to view image in full size

and in blobs/sha256 (first file) I found one “API_TOKEN” classic data leak via cache blobs.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Token looked base64-flavored, decoded it, and out came the flag.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Sometimes the “advanced forensics disk image” challenge is really just “did you check the obvious folder.” 🕵️
6. Mind Stone : Protocol Drift
The Mind Stone was once secured using a custom communication protocol developed by Vision’s architects. Following a suspected compromise, fragments of the protocol, encrypted data, and internal documentation were recovered. Analyse the artifacts, understand how the protocol operates, and recover the hidden secret used to protect the Mind Stone.

• Files Provided : mentioned below

• Category : Cryptography
so there were total four files
the dev’s rule open `README.txt` first so did I ,
then changelog.log a classic log file along with the timestamps
the encrypted.txt
and then the public.txt contained
finally protocol_v3.txt,
hmm now it makes some sense
From public.txt, pulled out the Diffie-Hellman parameters: g, p, A, B. A quick Google confirmed it was textbook DH, small prime, so this was crackable by hand (well, by dCode 😅).
Headed to dcode.fr to compute discrete logs
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Press enter or click to view image in full size

g = 5, y(A) = 8, p = 23 → x = 6
g = 5, y(B) = 19, p = 23 → x = 15then checked the Modular Exponentiation
Press enter or click to view image in full size

and got the shared secret as “2”
now from protocol_v3.txt the key derivation was laid out
K1 = SHA256(shared_secret)
= SHA256(2)
= d4735e3a265e16eee03f59718b9b5d03019c07d8b6c51f90da3a666eec13ab35K2 = SHA256(K1 + "O8!Uf()unD!t") #the leaked seed
so I wrote a python script to get the KEY
Press enter or click to view image in full size

then in CyberChef I set base64 > XOR with the HEX key and got the flag as output
Press enter or click to view image in full size

7. Power Stone : NOVA ACCESS
The Nova Corps secured access to the Power Stone using an RSA-based authentication system. Following a breach, investigators recovered a public key and traces of the authentication infrastructure. Intelligence suggests a weakness in the key generation process may have exposed access to a critical server. Analyze the provided artifacts, recover access, and claim the Power Stone.

• File Provided : id_rsa.zip

• Category : Cryptography + Networks
Given id_rsa.zip, containing what looks like an SSH public key, no private key in sight.
The Goal, recover the private key and use it to hop onto a remote service for the flag.
so after extracting the zip we got two things,

• id_rsa.pub.rtf : this is where the public SSH key was
Press enter or click to view image in full size

2. __MACOSX : where the MACOS meta data is stored
Press enter or click to view image in full size

here a hidden file was there .id_rsa.pub.rtf, tried to print that
Press enter or click to view image in full size

so firstly I need to extract the clean SSH Public Key, for that I copied the full key and created a file named “id_rsa.pub” used a utility as the ssh-keygen
Press enter or click to view image in full size

verified the file
Press enter or click to view image in full size

then exported the in pem
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Press enter or click to view image in full size

ran an extractor
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Tried a tool called RsaCTFTool
Press enter or click to view image in full size

now from the recovered private key we tried to login and got the FLAG.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

the Reverse Engineering ones (the rest of the Infinity Stones, fittingly) were cracked by my awesome teammates, because no one snaps their fingers alone. 🫰
